text:
This monograph examines the evolution of official communication in Upper Silesia in the era of the early modern state from the 16th to the 18th century using a discursological approach to explore the linguistic and legal diversity of this historic region. The monograph discusses issues such as the language of chancellery and judicial documents in a region that was not part of the Polish state for centuries and was under Czech and Prussian administration. The region’s rich archival heritage preserved to this day in cities such as Tarnowskie Góry, Woźniki, Chorzów, Mysłowice and Opole is presented in the context of communication structures and forms of interaction between local authorities and the subordinate local community.

Title page. Discourse in European Placement Law: Office and Court explores the communicative possibilities and constraints offered by the law in medieval and early modern Europe, through an examination of two distinct functional types of discourse found there: office (chancellery) discourse and judicial discourse. Office discourse is concerned with the validation and registration of legally significant events such as sales and wills, and develops from open descriptive narratives of the situation in play to highly formalised and highly structured genre forms. Judicial discourse is concerned with the arbitration of legally contentious events, and the enforcement of order in society, and it too develops highly formalised and highly structured discourse styles, comprising identifiable stages of complaint, response and judgement. The research focuses for the first time on the cognitive processes which medieval and early modern local scribes underwent in preparing the paperwork of government and the law, a process which saw the accommodation of highly formalised chancellery language styles and conventions with elements of everyday speech and judicial Latin. Discourse in European Placement Law: Office and Court identifies the extent to which discourse in medieval and early modern Europe has maintained continuity, and arguing that contemporary administrative and legal communication styles can be seen to be rooted in the medieval and early modern periods, with Upper Silesia playing a key role in the history of the Polish language.
